summary:
Arrays store elements in memory next to each other. They allow quick access but moving or removing items can be slow.

Linked lists have nodes with data and pointers to other nodes. They can change size easily, but you cannot jump directly to any part.

Stacks use LIFO (last in first out) for adding and removing items.

Queues use FIFO (first in first out).

Binary search works well on sorted arrays by dividing them in half.

Some questions test your knowledge of these data structures and algorithms.


Key Takeaways: Arrays have quick access but slow insertions. Linked lists can change size easily but do not allow direct access. Stacks follow LIFO, Queues follow FIFO. Binary search divides arrays to find items quickly.

hallucination.label: High Confidence — Summary is well grounded in document
